speaker: candidate
question: You mentioned you have system implementation experience and implement risk accordingly. Is that how you hit a middle ground so your message is consumable by both high-level and technical audiences?
text:
The Translation Effect: This is my unique expertise. When cascading risk, we use high-level "risk domains" (regulatory, operational, technological) to report to executive management.
Audience Adaptation: You cannot speak to an engineer using abstract legal terms. For example, I shouldn't talk to an engineer about "GDPR compliance risk" per se; I must translate it into the specific technological risk domains that overlay those GDPR requirements within their codebase.

speaker: interviewer
question: How does making decisions at a global level versus a single headquarters impact the speed, efficiency, and adoption rate of driving compliance work?
text:
Consensus vs. Speed: At Meta, leadership requires a bottom-up consensus model. If 10 people have 10 different opinions, you must find a middle ground, which creates a large turnaround time for decisions.
Hybrid Execution: TikTok is heavily execution-focused. It balances bottom-up input with a highly decisive top-down direction. Decisions and iterations happen blindingly fast.
Engineering per Capita: TikTok has a much higher concentration of engineers per function compared to Meta (e.g., where Meta allocates 1 engineer, TikTok may allocate 5). If you need compliance tooling or infrastructure built, the engineering teams will build it incredibly quickly.
The Catch: Because execution is so fast, your directions must be intensely specific and verbalized clearly. If you give them precise instructions, they will get it done immediately.

speaker: interviewer
question: Based on your two years here, how can an individual coming from an environment like Meta set themselves up for success in their first year?
text:
Drop the Big-Tech Ego: The biggest hurdle for hires coming from Meta, Google, or Apple is that they tend to keep themselves on a "different level" and fail to maintain an open mind. They assume their previous company's way is the only right way. TikTok does not operate like those companies.
Plug into the Global Network: You must immediately cross-functionalize (XFN) and align with your global network. Embrace, respect, and learn the working styles and perspectives of your colleagues in China and other global regions.

speaker: candidate
question: How do you approach a significant culture shift on a team, or guide them to operate differently if you notice their current effectiveness is lacking? Do you have a real-world example?
text:
Establish Clear Triggers: I never introduce change arbitrarily. I establish complete transparency around why the change is happening and ground it in a clear external or internal trigger.
Phased Rollout Strategy: I actively bring the team along through a phased evolution so they don't feel overwhelmed or reject the new processes outright.
The Meta Example (System Integration): * The Problem: At Meta, I led a program-based operational risk management team, while a parallel system was tracking risk from a technical product-based standpoint. They operated in distinct silos, leading to major duplication of effort. We needed to integrate both into a single, in-house system to streamline controls for audits.
The Resistance: The team was highly comfortable with their legacy, siloed tools. The new in-house tool had poor user experience (UX), causing severe pushback over feared slowdowns.
The Execution: I hosted interactive sessions to emphasize the long-term efficiency wins. Instead of forcing an overnight migration, we used a pilot phase. We left their high-frequency, everyday controls in the old system so daily output didn't stall. We migrated their low-frequency, secondary controls first to test and iterate on the new setup.
The Outcome: Though the system experienced stability issues early on, the phased pilot allowed us to "fail fast," gather direct feedback, optimize the tool with the engineering team, and smoothly evolve the team's operational habits.

speaker: candidate
question: When people react to change emotionally rather than logically-clinging to old ways of working out of anxiety-how do you handle well-intentioned team members who are in flat disagreement with your approach?
text:
Isolate the Root Concern: Behind emotional resistance is usually a practical, unaddressed fear. During the Meta integration, a senior team member aggressively pushed back, stating, "We are not implementing anything new for the next four years."
Empathy over Enforcement: I held a private 1-on-1 to look past the defiance and uncover the core issue: the team was already completely buried under a heavy review workload, and a chaotic wave of corporate layoffs/reorgs was causing intense job insecurity.
Collaborative Control Mapping: I validated his stress and used a hands-on exercise to shift his perspective. We sat down and mapped his current review workload against the new integrated system. I proved that instead of building privacy reviews from scratch every single time, the integrated model provided a "plug-and-play" framework for overlapping controls.
Trust Through Incremental Wins: By demonstrating tangible time-savings on a small subset of products, he slowly built trust in the system. Over time, as product volume scaled, the efficiency gains became undeniable.

speaker: candidate
question: With highly intelligent, technical teams, people often naturally gravitate toward projects that are technically fascinating or complex (like building in-depth risk models) rather than what the business actually needs. How do you set strict prioritization?
text:
The Three-Pronged Framework: At the start of every quarter, I explicitly anchor the team's roadmap to three non-negotiable vectors:
Regulatory Requirements
Customer Commitments
Business Goals / Operational Effectiveness
The Risk-Assessment Filter: When a team member or outside stakeholder aggressively demands an ad-hoc project (e.g., "We need to write a brand new data retention policy tomorrow"), I run it through a systematic filter: What is the objective trigger? What is the explicit risk to the business if we do not build this right now?
Defensive Bumping: If a project does not map to our quarterly OKRs, it is pushed back-unless an external signal (like an impending regulatory audit) shifts the urgency, in which case we defensively reprioritize.

speaker: candidate
question: This specific data protection team has been without a leader for months, survived a major corporate restructuring, and feels completely swallowed up by a massive Security umbrella org. They are working hard but feel unmoored. How do you handle this specific inheritance?
text:
No Preemptive Assumptions: When stepping into a low-morale team, I immediately launch individualized 1-on-1 loops. Even during a uniform corporate restructuring, every person processes change differently based on their unique career position.
Reinforce Individual Fit: I explicitly map their daily tasks back to the macroscopic corporate architecture. People lose motivation when they feel invisible; showing them exactly how their specific "puzzle piece" protects the broader business restores their sense of value.
Protect and Reassure Autonomy: I explicitly communicate to strong, senior personalities that my arrival is not a micro-management campaign. I intentionally preserve their autonomy, match them with high-visibility projects, and act purely as a servant leader to clear roadblocks.
Strategic Talent Shifting: Reorgs often force people into tasks that don't align with their technical strengths. As I actively rehire and scale the team, I intentionally reshape the chart-bringing in new talent to absorb operational gaps so legacy team members can return to doing their best work.

speaker: candidate
question: What is the career makeup of the groups you've led (early, mid, late career), and how do you adjust your coaching and management style for them?
text:
Early Career (New Graduates): Highly ambitious, high-energy, and looking to "take over the world." My role here is to harness that velocity, provide guardrails, and actively teach them how to navigate corporate dynamics.
Late Career / Stable Veterans: Often highly capable but content where they are. They may be navigating major life milestones (e.g., starting a family or dealing with an "empty nest"). I don't force aggressive vertical growth expectations on them if they aren't seeking it. Instead, I optimize for stability, reward their technical excellence, and keep their core workload highly predictable.
The "Manager-as-IC" Dynamic: Managing senior personnel who used to be leaders themselves requires high trust and clear boundaries. I lean heavily on asking open-ended questions: "How do you prefer to be led? What level of autonomy do you need from me to deliver your best work?"
Unlocking Career Blockers (Example): I had a high-performing mid-career IC who wanted to transition into people management, but the current team scope didn't provide standard manager headcount. To bridge this gap without changing corporate titles, I explicitly assigned all of our contingent/contract workers to report directly to him. This allowed him to build a verified track record of leadership that ultimately unlocked his career acceleration.

speaker: interviewer
question: Given your recent transition to this role over the last three months, how has your own leadership style had to change or evolve to adapt to this culture of high autonomy?
text:
Transitioning from Bureaucracy to High Context: Coming from a traditional, highly matrixed corporation like Disney (where I spent 16 years), my standard playbook was to have a detailed grasp of every project.
Letting Go of the Micro-Details: With a significantly larger, geographically distributed department, I've had to intentionally stop tracking fine details. When you have 6-10 co-located people, comprehensive tracking is easy. In a massive, remote, distributed tech ecosystem, you must rely entirely on your management tier.
The "Informed Leader" Concept: The culture relies on the concept of an Informed Captain (the person with ultimate autonomy over a project) and an Informed Leader. As a leader, I don't dictate solutions. I step back, listen, ask targeted diagnostic questions, and pull context from cross-functional partners to ensure my entire leadership team is moving in the exact same strategic direction.

speaker: interviewer
question: What specific non-obvious skills or qualities must the person stepping into this exact Data Protection role arm themselves with to be successful in their first year?
text:
The interviewer explicitly defines three core mandates for the incoming leader:
1. Shift from "Academic Risk" to "Business Drivers"
The legacy team is incredibly smart but overly academic. They build abstract, mathematically dense compliance frameworks (e.g., mapping out bloated, multi-layered data classification tiers) that stall operations. You must coach them to articulate risk using business metrics.
Instead of saying, "We must implement a data retention policy because of GDPR compliance risk," reframe it as: "Implementing data retention slashes storage costs, improves database performance query speeds, and drastically heightens internal data quality by purging stale records." You must connect risk to business survival and bottom-line protection.
2. Kill the Silo Mentality
Historically, this team operated in total isolation and treated compliance like a post-production checkpoint. They would wait until an engineering team built a system, and then "dump compliance requirements" on them without warning. When the product team inevitably responded with "we don't have the bandwidth," the requirement would die.
You must build deeply collaborative relationships with product units during early planning cycles. You need to transition the team to a model of empathetic compromise, ensuring engineering teams understand the risk tradeoffs clearly without feeling targeted.
3. Integrate Parallel Engineering Workflows
Security assurance teams within the broader org are already building software development lifecycle (SDLC) security review frameworks that heavily overlap with our data privacy reviews.
As Director, your immediate technical objective is to partner with the Privacy Infrastructure and Security Engineering managers to templatize and integrate these review processes. We must unify the tooling pipeline so external engineering teams face a singular, frictionless compliance checkpoint rather than dual, redundant reviews.

speaker: candidate
question: How do you deal with basically creating a culture shift on a team, or trying to guide them in a different direction, or change the way that they've been operating, if perhaps you're seeing any issues with the effectiveness of the team? If you have examples of things that you have had to change in the past, that would be great.
text:
Transparency of the Trigger: When changing how a team works, I ensure there is complete transparency regarding the trigger for that change. I don't just introduce change blindly; I set high context.
Bringing the Team Along: I integrate the team directly into all the different phases of the change process and preempt the operational impacts ahead of time, ensuring our view of the impact aligns with theirs.
The Meta Integration Example: At Meta, I led a compliance team performing risk management from a programmatic, process-oriented standpoint (evaluating operational and financial risk metrics). Concurrently, a parallel internal team was performing risk management from a technical, product-based standpoint. They were operating in two completely separate systems.
The Friction Point: We realized that the controls solving for process risks could also solve for product risks, resulting in a massive duplication of effort. We needed to integrate both systems to report controls uniformly for audits. However, the teams were deeply anxious because they were highly familiar with their legacy setup, and the internal tool Meta built in-house at the time had a very poor user experience (UX).
The Execution Strategy: I held sessions to explain why we needed to pivot and the long-term benefits we would obtain, actively securing their buy-in. To prevent daily work from stalling, we rolled the change out in distinct phases. We allowed them to maintain their high-frequency, everyday controls on the current legacy system. We then used their low-frequency, rarely modified controls to test and pilot the new integrated platform.
The Outcome: It wasn't entirely smooth-there were days where the system completely broke or shut down because users were manipulating different parts of the database incorrectly. However, the phased pilot gave us the cushion to "fail fast," collect feedback, push updates back to the tooling engineers, and allow the team to naturally evolve into the change.

speaker: candidate
question: What you were describing there... someone was just really pushing back. Even if you were trying to explain the objectives and they understood because they had been used to operating in a particular way. A lot of times people's reaction to change can be more emotional than logical... so how did you, either in that case or in another case, deal with a situation where well-intentioned people were essentially just in disagreement about the approach?
text:
Uncovering the Workload Panic: On that specific Meta integration project, we faced severe pushback. The underlying trigger for the emotion was operational: that specific team was already completely swamped with an intense volume of reviews they had to clear within tight timeframes. Introducing a brand-new system presented a massive risk of reducing their short-term efficiency.
The "Four-Year" Defiance: I remember a specific team member who flatly told me, "the candidate, this is not going to work. We are not implementing something new here, maybe in the next four years."
Empathy and Isolated Mapping: Instead of enforcing the rule, I sat down with him in a private 1-on-1 to understand his core concerns. He couldn't articulate it cleanly in the big group meeting, but his fear was pure workload efficiency. On top of that, Meta was going through layers of corporate uncertainty (layoffs/restructuring) at the time, and people were tying their work quality to job security, creating intense anxiety.
The Solution: I showed empathy, validated his workload stress, and did a practical exercise. I booked time with him and the team to do a physical mapping of their reviews. I proved to him that under the new integrated process, instead of writing new compliance controls from scratch, it would be purely "plug-and-play" for any overlapping risks.
Building Trust Gradually: While we didn't identify an overwhelming amount of overlapping controls on day one, as they began reviewing a wider variety of products over time, they realized just how many baseline controls they could reuse. This practical time-saving built trust, the emotional resistance faded, and the migration succeeded.

speaker: candidate
question: The example is particularly interesting to me because risk has been a big discussion with our data protection team, specifically regarding the prioritization of what they should be working on. People here are very excited about developing highly in-depth, complex risk models, which might not actually be necessary... How do you deal with the prioritization of work on a team? Especially when you have bright people, there is often a desire to work on things that are more technically interesting or challenging. How do you balance getting the prioritization set based on business needs versus the technical input you're getting from your team?
text:
The Three Priorities Baseline: At the start of every single quarter, I ground my team by setting clear goals anchored exclusively to three pillars: Regulatory Requirements, Customer Commitments, and Business Goals / Operational Effectiveness.
Filtering Ad-Hoc Requests: Bright teams constantly come back from cross-functional meetings with shiny new ideas. For example, a team member might say, "I was talking to a stakeholder team and they want us to build a comprehensive data retention policy tomorrow." My immediate response is to filter it: Why do they need that? What is the objective trigger? What is the explicit business risk if we don't do this right now?
Managing Deviations via Risk Registers: Many things sound hyper-important in the moment, but we always anchor back to our baseline quarterly goals. If we must deviate from our roadmap, it has to pass a risk assessment. For instance, if we receive a clear signal that an audit is coming up in a specific environment, that item is legitimately bumped to a P0 priority to ensure we are defensive on our regulatory compliance. Otherwise, the team stays focused on the baseline goals.

speaker: candidate
question: Have you done a lot of OKR planning in terms of your team and larger groups? I'm curious how you manage that, given that data protection or privacy is often a combination of inputs into other teams (like asking them to do retention) but also receiving requests from other teams (like engineering asking for guidance on how to architect a new data warehouse). How do you balance and align those different objectives across groups to make sure privacy is addressing business needs, managing risk, and keeping all stakeholders informed, since it can get quite complicated?
text:
Upstream Executive Sign-off: When I was at Meta, our quarterly business goals and roadmaps were shared with and vetted by our key cross-functional stakeholder leaders before we signed off on them. We brought these stakeholder leaders into the room during the planning phase to ensure their technical needs were directly accounted for in our upcoming roadmap.
The Crisis Handling Framework (Short-Term vs. Long-Term): To handle emergency requests or "firefighting" that lands outside the roadmap, you must balance resource constraints against regulatory urgency.
The Meta Sensitive Data Exposure Example: We experienced an incident at Meta where sensitive employee data within the People organization was exposed-thousands of internal users had access permissions to files they never should have seen. The team rushed to us demanding an immediate, comprehensive access control policy.
The Execution: Because we had an impending regulatory audit looking specifically at access controls, this emergency request was instantly fast-tracked. However, because our timeline was incredibly short, we couldn't draft and deploy an enterprise-wide access policy overnight without breaking systems.
The Trade-Off Approach: We broke the solution into bite-sized milestones. We created temporary, short-term processes and procedures for the immediate data environment to satisfy the incoming audit, while explicitly pushing the comprehensive, long-term policy documentation into the next quarter's budget. We use this explicit trade-off approach with stakeholders: if we cannot fit an item into the current quarter due to bandwidth, we guarantee them a slot in the next quarter so they feel secure.

speaker: candidate
question: How have you dealt with situations where there's basically low morale or a lack of motivation on a team? I'll be very transparent: this organization I am leading was moved into a larger Security, Privacy, and Assurance group about 6 or 7 months ago, right before I started. There was a massive reorg, and this group (PDPE) feels like they were completely swallowed up by a much larger organization. On top of that, they haven't had a leader for a while-their Director left, they went months without management, and I only stepped in a couple of months ago. The team seems a little unmoored. If you have inherited a team through org changes and had to right the ship in terms of motivation and morale, how did you handle it?
text:
Individualized Discovery over Assumptions: I have navigated this exact scenario. At Meta, we went through massive waves of layoffs that resulted in aggressive, constant team reorganizations. I inherited a team suffering from deep low morale, which was immediately obvious during our group meetings. My first rule is that I never make assumptions about the uniform cause of low morale; individuals respond to corporate trauma differently depending on their unique personal situations.
Reinforcing Value in the Puzzle: I initiate immediate 1-on-1 loops to understand what "best work" and "impact" mean to each person individually. When a team goes leaderless and gets swallowed by a larger group, their primary psychological fear is: "Am I still valuable to this company, or am I going to be cut?" I explicitly connect their daily technical tasks back to the macro-level goals of the company, showing them exactly where their specific puzzle piece fits into the grand corporate picture.
Reassuring Autonomy to Strong Personalities: For senior practitioners who have operated without a manager for months, they fiercely fear losing their independence. I explicitly look them in the eye and reinforce that I am not here to steal their autonomy. In fact, I focus on giving them more autonomy and securing them higher visibility across the new, larger org.
The Servant Leader Reshuffle: Reorgs frequently force high-performing people into taking on messy, leftover operational tasks that they despise or that don't map to their technical strengths. As a servant leader, I use my hiring power to fix this. When I rehire and scale the team, I intentionally recruit new talent to fill those operational gaps, allowing my legacy team members to hand off the work they hate and return to their true technical strengths. This structural relief is what ultimately restores team morale.

speaker: candidate
question: In terms of the types of people that you've managed, where have they been in their career? Is it early-career, mid-career, late-career... what's kind of the makeup of the groups you've led, and how do you adjust your coaching and management style for them?
text:
Early-Career (Recent College Graduates): This is where I started my management journey as an engineering manager. They are highly ambitious, high-velocity, and view the world as something they want to take over. My coaching style here is to channel that raw energy, keep them intensely driven, and teach them how to navigate complex corporate structures without burning out.
Late-Career / The "Empty Nesters": These are stable, highly experienced veterans who have reached a point in their career where they feel comfortable and capped. They aren't looking for aggressive corporate advancement or vertical ladder-climbing; they might be focused on starting families, or conversely, their kids have left the house and they are dealing with "empty nest" dynamics. My management style shifts to stabilizing their environment. I ensure their workload is highly predictable, honor their technical mastery, and do not force growth metrics on them that they do not want, provided their daily execution remains excellent.
The "Manager-turned-IC" Dynamic: Managing senior professionals who used to run teams themselves requires high boundaries. I remove micro-management entirely and lean heavily on listening. I ask them directly: "How do you personally prefer to be led? What communication cadence do you need from me to maintain your operational autonomy?" I balance the growth of the business with their personal expectations of themselves.

speaker: candidate
question: Have you had situations where, in terms of management style, you've struggled to figure out what works for people? Because I quite frankly have. People often say they want certain things, but they might really want something else. How do you get people to tell you what they really want, and how do you sit down with them to create a balanced effect of growth when career titles might be capped?
text:
Testing Unspoken Desires: People frequently hide their true career desires behind generic corporate statements like, "I just want to do great on this job." They can't iterate what "great" actually means. My strategy is to test different growth angles, separating business impact from personal career milestones.
The Aspiring People Manager Example: I managed a senior team member who was executing flawlessly-his peers loved him and his technical quality was exceptional. However, he confessed in a 1-on-1 that he felt professionally stagnant because his true goal was to pivot into people management, but our immediate team chart didn't have open manager headcount.
Creating Synthetic Headcount: To give him genuine growth without changing his official corporate title, I leveraged our contingent/contract worker pipeline. I restructured the team so that all incoming contingent vendors reported directly to him. This allowed him to practice real-world people management, run standups, and handle resource allocation. When a formal manager opening eventually appeared on a sister team, I proudly put him forward with a proven leadership track record, and he secured the role.

speaker: interviewer
question: Leading with Autonomy as an "Informed Leader"
text:
The Transition from Detailed Control: In every previous role, my playbook during the first 90 days was to listen, run 1-on-1s, and get a highly detailed, granular grasp of every single project under my jurisdiction. When you manage a small, co-located team of 6 to 10 people pre-COVID, walking out of your office and joining casual conversations makes detailed tracking simple.
The Distributed Reality: Here, I am managing a vastly larger, completely remote, and geographically distributed department. There are hundreds of localized tools and legacy systems that have been built over years that I am still technically learning about. I cannot physically know all the details anymore. I have to rely completely on my management layer to ensure we are traveling in the same direction.
The Informed Captain Framework: The culture here relies on the concept of the Informed Captain (the individual practitioner who owns full autonomy over a project's execution) and the Informed Leader. As an informed leader, I don't micro-manage or dictate guidance early.
Uncovering Hidden Disagreements: For example, I recently noticed an engineering policy direction that I fundamentally felt was wrong and needed a complete revamp. Being new, I held my tongue, took my time, and simply asked targeted diagnostic questions to understand the baseline background. Because I leaned into context over control, the engineers finally opened up and admitted, "Yeah, we actually don't agree with this policy either, but this is just how it was historically started." If I had entered with a heavy bureaucratic hand, I would have faced walls; leading with context allowed us to naturally align on a revamp.

speaker: interviewer
question: Fostering Business Partnerships over Isolated Frameworks
text:
The interviewer explicitly details the three core operational flaws the incoming leader must fix:
1. Eradicating Academic Compliance Bloat
Our team is incredibly smart, but their architectural output has been completely academic. They design highly idealized, bloated data classification frameworks with far too many layers that completely stall business velocity. You must coach this team to think about compliance through the lens of business outcomes.
Do not allow them to use terrifying, rigid risk terminology with the business. When implementing data retention, don't just pitch it as a GDPR legal mandate. Reframe it directly to the product teams as a business driver: "Data retention purges old, stale records, which directly slashes our cloud storage costs, drastically heightens our internal data quality, and accelerates database query performance."
2. Stopping the "Requirement Dumping" Silo
Historically, this team operated in a total silo. They would perform isolated risk reviews and then "dump their compliance requirements" onto a product team's lap at the very end of the lifecycle. The product team would look at the unexpected checklist, say, "We don't have the engineering bandwidth for this in our OKRs," and completely ignore it.
You must break this silo. You need to build deep, empathetic relationships with the business units early so that compliance is a collaborative negotiation based on mutual risk trade-offs, not a corporate mandate that people take personally when dropped.
3. Unifying Parallel Engineering Reviews
Right now, we have massive overlap. We have a decentralized Privacy Engineering team and a Security Assurance team running independent software development lifecycle (SDLC) security reviews that ask for almost the exact same data as our privacy reviews.
Your immediate mandate is to sit down with the Privacy Infrastructure Engineering managers to templatize, automate, and integrate our review processes. We must combine these checkpoints so that our internal engineering partners face a singular, unified compliance pipeline rather than being passed between redundant internal groups.

speaker: candidate
question: Netflix culture emphasizes "people over process" and "context not control." Project planning is fluid, not standard. With so much freedom, how do you ensure your team is working on what is most important to the company?
text:
Proactive Goal Setting: I set scope, goals, and objectives ahead of time so they are crystal clear to both my team and cross-functional stakeholders.
Outcome Alignment: We align closely on the specific department goals, success metrics, and expected outcomes of the broader organization.
Translation into Action: We translate those high-level objectives into clear deliverables and everyday tasks. This transparency keeps the team focused on high-value items while maintaining stakeholder alignment within a given timeframe.

speaker: candidate
question: How do you stay aligned with the direction of the company, especially in a very fast-paced environment?
text:
Anchoring to Core Objectives: While the tech and regulatory landscapes (like at Meta) change rapidly, we remain anchored to our set baseline goals.
Phased Risk-Assessments: When a pivot is triggered, I carry out a risk assessment to measure the operational impact of the change and evaluate whether to roll it out fully or in phases.
Proactive Signal Spotting (Example): At Meta, before employee data privacy became a regulatory priority in the US, we picked up signals from our UK/legal teams that EU regulators would shift focus there. We proactively built requirements for employee data. Years later, when the EU and New York passed laws regarding employee data and AI, we were already prepared.
Scalability & Communication: I focus on building scalable programs that natively accommodate change, and then communicate shifts immediately.

speaker: candidate
question: How do you define and measure the success of your team during program implementation that evolves over time?
text:
The candidate outlines three primary metrics (plus one bonus metric):
Timelines (Agility): Measuring how quickly and nimbly the team pivots and implements changes when triggered, focusing on improving velocity over time.
Workflow Impact: Evaluating how smoothly changes are absorbed into our workflows-and critically, how those adjustments impact our cross-functional stakeholders' operations.
Stakeholder Feedback: Actively gathering direct feedback from partner teams on how the pivot affected them and how effectively we collaborated.
Bonus Metric (Business Value): Assessing how our technical compliance pivots ultimately protect or drive the operational effectiveness and overarching business goals of the company.

speaker: candidate
question: How do you approach developing a strategy specifically for team performance and team growth?
text:
Transparent 1-on-1s: My foundational strategy is clear, two-way communication. I establish regular 1-on-1s with all direct reports to deeply understand their short- and long-term career aspirations.
Milestone-Driven Mapping: We map their goals directly to relevant projects. We don't leave career growth abstract; we set firm milestones (ranging from 1-month milestones in fast consulting setups to 3-to-6-month milestones in-house).
Bi-Directional Feedback Loop: I monitor progress against milestones and provide candid feedback. Concurrently, I ask for transparent feedback on my leadership style and whether I am supporting them effectively.

speaker: candidate
question: Can you describe a situation where your team was heading toward one strategic goal, but the business or external factors forced you to pivot directions completely?
text:
The Scenario: While leading the FTC mandate implementation team at Meta, our original scope was strictly defined as auditing data stored on-premise (within internal infrastructure). However, regulators explicitly shifted expectations mid-stream, demanding we evaluate data in the cloud.
The Pivot Plan:
Reprioritization: Evaluated the newly expanded scope and adjusted deliverables based on a risk-based metric.
Skill-to-Task Mapping: Assessed the team's strengths to align individuals with cloud infrastructure auditing.
Resource Gap Identification: Recognizing my team consisted primarily of program/product managers lacking deep cloud architecture experience, I requested and secured specialized engineering resources.
Stakeholder Alignment: Kept communication constant to ensure our new deployment strategy perfectly matched what both the business and regulators required.

speaker: candidate
question: What role does risk play in strategy development for you?
text:
Pragmatic Impact Analysis: Risk assessment brings practicality to strategy. It allows us to view impact from both sides: what can go wrong (threats) and what can go right (opportunities).
Framework for Prioritization: A risk-based lens explicitly defines:
Urgency: What happens if we do this now vs. later?
Level of Effort: Balancing complexity against cost.
External/Customer Focus: For customer-centric tech companies, risk mapping ensures we don't just optimize for internal operational ease, but heavily account for data privacy impacts on end-users.

speaker: candidate
question: Can you tell me about a time you went into a situation with very vague requirements or problem statements, but were still able to drive alignment and execution?
text:
The Scenario: An internal incident occurred at Meta involving sensitive employee data exposure, where individuals had access rights they shouldn't have had. The requirements given to the privacy team were incredibly vague regarding data sensitivity, true impact, or what policies/tech stack to implement.
The Execution Framework:
Root-Cause Trigger: I stepped back from the chaos to figure out the exact trigger of the issue and focused the team entirely on the core problem we wanted to solve.
Stakeholder Mapping: Identified and interviewed key players to discover that the root cause was a total lack of access control policies or standardized technical documentation for that specific environment.
Uncovering Hidden Requirements: Through deep stakeholder communication, we discovered an impending audit that would sweep this data ecosystem. This realization dictated our timeline and urgency.
Delivering Controls: We translated the ambiguous problem into a phased rollout: layer one was setting policies, standards, and documentation; layer two was implementing technical access controls to mitigate risk ahead of the audit.

speaker: candidate
question: What do you value most in a leader? Can you share an example of a past leader you admired?
text:
Radical Candor & Actionable Feedback: I highly value leaders who provide prompt, transparent, and direct feedback-both positive and negative-without ambiguous corporate cushion.
Context Over Control: I value leaders who provide high context rather than micro-managing, which fosters an environment of mutual trust.
The Example: A partner/mentor at Deloitte exemplified this. She gave swift, brutal, yet constructive feedback. She operated on the principle of "fail fast so you can win fast," empowering the team to make rapid, calculated decisions rather than stalling out due to fear of failure. She also led and influenced cross-functional stakeholders through trust and transparency rather than corporate compulsion or hierarchy.

speaker: candidate

question: For many employees at Strava, interacting with you will be their first time dealing with a strict compliance function. What is your approach to making regulatory requirements and legal processes feel less intimidating and more accessible?
text:
The Intersection of Compliance and Efficiency: I don't frame compliance as a set of rules meant to slow down production. I teach teams that robust compliance directly drives operational effectiveness.
The Record of Processing Activities (RoPA) Example: * If you tell an engineering team they must build a RoPA under EU law, they will find it bureaucratic and confusing.
Instead, I reframe it: "A RoPA is a comprehensive map and inventory of our entire data ecosystem." * When engineers view it as an end-to-end data map, they instantly see its practical engineering value: it highlights architectural vulnerabilities, exposes data pipeline bloat, and provides a direct reference point for tracking down infrastructure threats.

speaker: candidate
question: Connecting compliance to personal success is a great strategy. Are there any other core management principles you lean on to bring resistant teams along?
text:
I rely heavily on Strategic Reverse-Storytelling-taking the team from the commercial "endgame" back to the baseline engineering requirement.
Reframing the Narrative: For user-centric tech companies that handle mass consumer data, the ultimate goal of compliance is to scale customer trust.
The Trust vs. Fear Framework: I ask the product developers a simple baseline question: "When users open the feature you are building today, do you want them to interact with it out of trust, or out of fear?"
Plotting the Privacy Roadmap: Once they choose trust, we trace that narrative backward. To build trust, we must be transparent about data lifecycle usage. To ensure transparency, we must execute Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) to understand our data lifecycle. This ties their passion for user experience directly to our compliance checkpoints.

speaker: candidate
question: What specific actions do you take to encourage psychological safety? How do you ensure employees feel entirely comfortable raising red flags or calling out compliance violations?
text:
I build safety by operationalizing a strict communication matrix focused on four pillars:
Pillar
Operational Definition
Clarity
Eliminating corporate sentiment or fluff. Delivering objective realities about risks while giving team members the space to challenge and confirm definitions.
Timeliness
Shifting compliance upstream. Teams should never hear about a legal violation after the code is deployed; they must be pulled in early so they are empowered to architect the fix.
Transparency
Leading by example. Admitting what I do not know as a leader, showing total transparency about compliance outcomes, and modeling the exact behavior I expect from the engineering teams.
Inclusivity of Thought
Actively policing the room. In compliance debates, aggressive, loud voices often drown out less dominant thoughts. I intentionally pause meetings to draw out quieter team members who are still working through complex logic in their heads.

speaker: candidate
question: What is your philosophy or working framework for collaborating with key stakeholder groups like Community Management, Trust & Safety, Product, and Operations?
text:
Distinguishing Communication Style from Preference: I map out how my peers communicate versus how they prefer to be communicated with. Recognizing this distinction allows me to uncover the true intent and pain points behind their pushback.
Aligning on the Shared Goal: I eliminate territory friction immediately. I clarify to existing teams: "I am not here to take over your process or audit your past output; I am an ally here to enhance your operational safety and give you full credit for the work you've established."
Focusing on Impact over Role: I don't give cross-functional partners a boring list of roles and responsibilities. I frame their work as an impact driver.
Standard Approach: "Your role is to enforce privacy-by-design templates on this product."
My Approach: "Your impact on this project ensures we launch a bulletproof product that actively protects Strava's global brand reputation."
The Pre-Meeting Buy-In: Before entering a massive, formal cross-functional alignment meeting, I leverage my standing relationships to conduct private 1-on-1s with key decision-makers. Solving points of friction privately prevents public meetings from devolving into adversarial stalemates.

speaker: candidate
question: Let's talk about a major friction point. Our current Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) process is incredibly drawn out, and my team has been waiting weeks for a project to move forward. How do you streamline a broken compliance pipeline?
text:
I encountered this exact issue in a past role where executing a full DPIA for every minor product release was paralyzing development. I deployed a Two-Tiered Triage Framework to fix the pipeline:
                  [ Incoming Product / Feature ]
                                |
                  [ Automated Intake Assessment ]
                   (Data Type, Source, Retention)
                                |
                    +-----------+-----------+
                    |                       |
            [ Low / Medium Risk ]     [ High Risk ]
                    |                       |
            (Fast-Track Route)       (Full DPIA Route)
            • One-Week Turnaround    • Comprehensive Audit
            • Pre-Mapped Controls    • Multi-Month Timeline
            • Plug-and-Play Fixes    • Deep-Dive Security Mapping
1. The Automated Fast-Track Tier (Low/Medium Risk)
We built an automated intake questionnaire evaluating specific data points: What data types are you collecting? Is it third-party data? How long is it retained? If the responses generate a low-to-medium risk score based on architectural risks we have already solved in the past, the product bypasses the manual queue. The system automatically populates a set of pre-mapped, plug-and-play controls ("safe grounds") for the engineers to implement. This shrinks the turnaround time from months to one week.
2. The Full DPIA Tier (High Risk)
Only highly complex, unprecedented data-handling features trigger a full, manual DPIA audit.
3. Transparent SLA Communication
We openly publish these paths to the product teams. If they see that a 100% features rollout triggers a high-risk score (5-month timeline), but a phased, sandboxed MVP rollout triggers a low-risk score (1-week timeline), they are empowered to modify their own product roadmaps upstream. This aligns compliance directly with business velocity.

speaker: interviewer
question: How would you describe the current privacy culture at Strava? Is it highly mature and universally accepted, or is it still growing through an early stage of adoption?
text:
The Historical Context: Strava started off with a highly open platform philosophy. Years ago, as a woman on the team, I had to fight for privacy features because the early product was built from a very narrow demographic perspective that completely overlooked how diverse users wanted to protect their safety.
The Executive Wind: We have come a massive way since then, largely driven by public news cycles highlighting the real-world safety implications of fitness tracking data. Our new CEO is fiercely supportive, explicitly stating that Trust and Safety is our core business and cannot be compromised.
The Fragmented Reality: While the appetite for privacy is high, execution is entirely decentralized. Every individual product team is siloed and independently responsible for their own privacy guardrails. There is no centralized, top-down comprehensive view of data privacy across the entire platform, which makes tracking compliance highly complex and tricky.

speaker: interviewer
question: How would you describe leadership engagement and direction-setting at Strava? Is it top-down enforcement or bottom-up innovation?
text:
Silo-Dependent Autonomy: It varies wildly by business unit. Horizontal support and operational teams (like Community Management/Trust & Safety) are given intense autonomy. We make our own operational decisions and fight for scaling resources up to the executive level.
The Product Loop: For core product development, it is a hybrid loop. A small group of executive leaders and the board define the high-level macroscopic roadmap and business strategy. That strategy is circulated down to individual teams, who interpret it, build out detailed feature maps, and send them back up the chain for final executive sign-off. However, if the board or executive team shifts focus, the product teams must pivot immediately.

question: Is there a particular skill or interest you have that you want to explore that you haven't had the opportunity to do at Meta? Or is it more about just staying in the privacy sphere and seeing what else you can kind of get your hands on?
